request_body.prompt:
<|repo_name|>private-videos
<|file_sep|>instructions.txt
General project code rules:
- Never add comments to the end of a line.
- NEVER add TODO comments for me.
<|file_sep|>global.md
## General Code Preferences

- Avoid ambiguity, for example:

```lua
-- 👎 if there's a bug in the regex, how would I know? I'd have to surmise from surrounding code 🤮
if line:match("^%s*$") then
    -- ...
end
```

```lua
-- 🙌 clear intent
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```
<|file_sep|>wes_notes/evolution/2_users-do-not-edit-operations.md
<|fim_prefix|># TODOs

## TODO

- NOTE names below are for thinking through things, not necessarily used in code
- Outstanding operations to `Remove` the `Results` of prior `Op`erations
  - Users think in terms of previous operations (and want to edit the results)
    - Thus, we are _Removing the **Result** of Materialized Operations_
    - NOT removing, NOR undoing _arbitrary_ operations in the past
    - `Result` currently includes `Clips`
      - arguably also includes `Splits` (albeit we're trying out auto rejoin so we don't track the Split to undo, so in a way we are implicitly tracking Splits or at least implicitly undoing Splits)
      - later might include `Overlays` though we could classify those as a RemoveResultOfOverlayOp to isolate (and possibly add generic handling for all overlay operations)
    - naming:
      - `OpResult` - let's formalize the difference between an operation (`Op`) and its result (`OpResult`)?!
      - `RemoveResultOf___Op` vs `Remove___OpResult` 
        - start with `Remove Result Of ___ Op` to make it "readably" clear that we're _removing the result_, and _not_ accidentally think we're _removing the operation_.
    - FYI, understand that `cutting` works for some operation results like from `PauseOp` but not others like `CutOp` itself!
      - I am not sure if we want to standardize what remove does... if we standardize what was done (the op result) then maybe 
        - also possible we want a custom operation per `OpResult` type, and also possible there's some overlap in `OpResult`s between different operations
          we shall see, keep an open mind
  - Scenarios to explore:
    - [ ] Remove a Single Pause (let's just backup and cover this separate of nested pause, I have a hunch it will provide clarity)
        1. `InsertMediaOp`
        2. `PauseOp` (result is optional Split + Insert HoldClip... think of **`PauseOpResult`** or `ResultOfPauseOp`)
        3. **`RemoveResultOfPauseOp`**
    - [WIP] Remove a Nested Pauses Entirely
        1. `InsertMediaOp`
        2. `PauseOp`
        3. `PauseOp` (nested)
        4. remove each of the `ResultOfPauseOp`s in any order => materializes verbatim (rejoined) SourceClip from #1
    - [ ] `RemoveResultOfCutOp` - **DO THIS NEXT b/c it has diff approach vs RemoveResultOfPauseOp**
      Steps:
        1. `InsertMediaOp` materializes SourceClip1 
        2. `CutOp` materializes SourceClip1_left, Split, Removed SourceClip2_middle, Split, SourceClip2_right
           ... aside: UI wise this should show as a vertical line (screenpal does red dashed) and when the playhead is on it, you can trigger RemoveCutOp (next)
        3. `RemoveResultOfCutOp` => should rejoin and materialize original SourceClip1
           FYI this is where remove doesn't work as just CutOp!
           when CutOp is originally applied, how about we track what was removed?
           We could leave a zero-width clip in a Track...
             this might be the special Track I was mentioning that we show in the UI as a single Track that holds physical represntations of materialized elements that can be removed or modified
             we don't need anything in the tracks we use to export/playback, this is purely for editing only
             `EditsTrack`? `EditElementsTrack`? `EditCuesTrack`? ... better ideas?
               b/c `Edits` are what users would want to "remove" (approximately an undo but not undoing the operation, just undoing what is left of a past edit result)
             ALSO, this track can hold the groups a complex NarrateOp result "clip" as a single UI block over the span of what was narrated (simplified way to present what was done without breakign out A/V tracks and the myriad of underrun/overrun fragments)
    - [ ] `RemoveResultOfNarrateOp`
      - good test of what is probably the most complex result of any operation 
      Steps:
        1. `InsertMediaOp`
        2. `NarrateOp` (middle of SourceClip1)
        3. `<|fim_suffix|>


- TODO **should materialize handle the rejoin?**
  - see nested_pause test case
    - where I mark `materializes SourceClip1`
    - and NOT `materializes SourceClip1_left, SourceClip2_right`
  - Why not handle this in one spot, one time? When we apply the "RemoveClipOp"
    - Only attempt rejoin on the clips right before and the clips right after the applied operation? (not entire track)
    - do not rely on timeline consumers to run this
    - why not minimize differences between model of clips that the user sees and what we have under the hood?
    - TODO are there times we don't want to rejoin? Not sure if this is a thing? just hunch that I should consider it before assuming it is always ok to do
  - TODO is it possible ScreenPal is tracking Splits (like Clips/Overlays) and removing them to rejoin?
    is there a feasible way to do that across nested pause ops (for example)?

---


# Original ideas that led to => **Users edit operation results, not operations**

## Rationale

- The user is editing the `materialized timeline`
- Therefore they are editing the `clips` that result from applying operations
- They **do not edit the operations**!
  Some edits seem to be editing an Op but they're not, they're always editing the clips that result
    later we'll see that editing overlays are not editing the original OverlayOps but rather editing the Overlay that results from applying an OverlayOp!
    but that is not what's happening
  THEREFORE there is no such thing as Undoing or Removing a Past Operation short of undoing the Full OpLog back to that point!!

## Insert Pause

- InsertOp => PauseOp(1sec) 
  => CutOp(middle of _clip from Pause_) ... leave non-zero duration of pause clip on both sides
    => RemovePauseOp(left side) => right remains
    => RemovePauseOp(right side) => left remains
  => OR NarrateOp(middle of _clip from Pause_) ... also leve non-zero duration on both sides 
  => OR PauseOp(middle of _clip from PauseOp_)
     now we have three clips (makes sense!) as that is what materializes in the final timeline after applying all ops
       left of PauseOp1, PauseOp2, right of PauseOp1

  OVERLAYS
  => OR FreezeFrameOp(middle of _clip from PauseOp_)
     in this case, there's only 1 pause clip and it can be removed as one
     this lends credence to my idea that FreezeFrame is an Overlay Edit (not a timeline/track edit)
     SAME FOR 
     - OverlayArrowOp, OverlayShapeOp, OverlayTextOp, etc
     - HideCursorOp, 



  after cutting the Pause, there are now Two Distinct Clips that are first class User Experience
  the user is ALWAYS thinking in terms of the materialized timeline(track) positions as of the last operation
  - there is no notion of undoing a past operation, what would that even mean? 
    sure, it's simple to imagine when there are no subsequent edits that affect a prior operation
    but falls apart when there are edits that overlap
- CopyOp (copy across range that had Pause+NarrateOp above... and in spal it is all flattened into one media clip
  - has Remove for pasted Clip
  - if you CutOp the middle => it turns into two clips to remove

## OverlayOp?

We probably want to structure `OverlayOp(Op)` to contrast `ClipOp(Op)` (better name?)
- `ClipOp`s result in changes to `Clip(s)` (add/remove/modify)
  `AddClipOp` (singular in the `InsertMediaClipOp`?)
  `ModifyClipOp` => `ReplaceVideoClipOp` and `ReplaceAudioClipOp`
- `OverlayOp`s result in changes to `Overlay(s)` (add/remove/modify.. modify might just be remove+add)
  hrmm... so `AddOverlayOp` vs `RemoveOverlayOp` vs `ModifyOverlayOp` ?
    RemoveOverlayOp might be a single Op with no descendents
      remove is probably universal to all overlay types
    AddOverlayOp would have one child per overlay type
- we may have other Op types, not aware of any, but wouldn't surprise me

UX
- I think I want a separate visual channel for showing Overlays (not OverlayOp, Overlays that result from the OverlayOp)



## PROMPT

ok so, I spent some time meditating on what it means to "remove" and I am creeping into "edit/modify" too...

I busted out some videos in ScreenPal and walked through various edits (i.e. InsertPause => Cut middle => Cut Pause right side => Cut Pause left side) and this is when it hit me...
- first off I have to say, thank god you formalized `materialize` as that immediately resonates with me and I've had this tickling in my subconscious for a few days now...
- at first, it felt wrong to think that users would edit the materialized timeline... but that is EXACTLY WHAT THEY DO... that is what I love about ScreenPal... and really all NLEs
- I expect WHAT I SEE IS WHAT I GET (when I do an export)... that is core to Previewing! and it's the core of Editing too!
- I expect to edit the materialized clips (and overlays) as of the last operation... not any prior operation (except its impact on the state of the current materialized timeline)

Therefore, do you see where I am going with this? 

It makes zero sense to talk about removing a past operation! because... users do not edit Operations... they edit Clips and Overlays (the _materialized_ result of Operations)!!!!

I suspect this is fundamental, where we've been heading w/o even knowing we were destined to arrive here!

Restated.. `ClipOp(Op)` is an operation that modifies the Clips on the Timeline/Tracks... when it is applied with `materilize()` we get out a set of clips

Generally speaking `Op` is an accounting mechanism to support `Undo`/`Redo`, 
- operations are largely transparent to users other than that is how you can express past actions taken
- and, ClipOp is the Verb, the Noun is the result which is the Clips!

And then, we've both had this feeling for a while now... Overlays are special, secondary... a second pass (most likely).. they sit on top of the Clips! 
- so we also have `OverlayOp(Op)` which is a family of operations that operate on `Overlays`
- users will then remove/modify the `Overlays` but not the `OverlayOps`!

I know of no Ops currently that operate on both, but it wouldn't be hard to imagine a composite edit tool... 
- in fact our AIEditOperation is very likely going to compose many Ops! that's an aside

So I started to think about what could we do to group within the ClipOp and OverlayOp ... we really are struggling to move from AddClipOps into RemoveClipOp and later ModifyClipOp...
- it very well may help to think in simple terms of Adding, Removing and Modifying.. 
- not sure those settle into final types, but categorically it can help us think higher level about families of tools instead of individual tools 
  - I know my dodo bird brain has trouble juggling all of the Ops... 
  - in large part because I had no mechanism to group them... <|fim_middle|>
